speaker: interviewer
text: Is that door closed all the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ira?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] second.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is February 23, 1996. We are speaking to Mark Zilist, M-A-R-K, Z-I-L-I-S-T. This is tape two. Mark, you were telling us how you hid in the hay. The farmer's driving his wagon. And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got to the outskirts of the town, I jumped off the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: The town was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrog. And I walked to town. I'd been on-- I'd been in that town before with the farmer that I worked for. But unbeknown to me, where I'm going to be sleeping the next night. I wound up on an open-air market. And I was roaming around. And the-- when I got there, the first thing I saw is a truck pulling up between two Poles. And they hung a man. The Russians hung a man. And it kind of scared me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I still had a lot of confidence in the Russians that I'm not going to be hurt. So I saw it. And I was upset. And I kept on walking around in the open-air market, until this lady came to me. [DRINKS] She says, you Jewish? I said, yes. This lady came to you and said, are you Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She said, are you Jewish? I says, yes. She says, what's your name? I says, Volodka. She says, that's not a Jewish name. But come with me. Come with me. She took me in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: In her house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In her house. And she took me in a room and gave me a pail of water and some clean clothes and a towel. And she gave me clean clothes from her husband. There was sizes bigger than what I needed. But I managed to clean up and put on some clean clothes. And she gave me something to eat. And then she started asking me where I was from and what happened to me. And I explained to her. And I told her that I was-- and she start talking to me in Jewish a little bit. And I understand-- understood just some words. Because I was away for it for a few years. At any rate, she realized that I was a Yiddish boy survived. And she put me up. She kept me in the house. And she told-- her two oldest daughters slept in the living room. They had-- I believe that was a one-bedroom house. They slept in the living room. And she told her two daughters to sleep on the floor that night [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [DRINKS] and let me sleep in the bed. Because I haven't slept in bed for a long period of time. After that, she told me that she's going to keep me. And she's going to take care of me, and made me part of the family. And from there, from Ostrog, we went to-- go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mark, did she have a husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a husband and three daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And three daughters?

speaker: interviewee
text: The story behind this--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The story behind this is she survived with her whole family on a farm in a-- in a hole dug in the ground. She was in that hole for 16 or 18 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole family. The hole was covered up with-- with straw and lumber, even with the ground, that it was not noticeable. The lady from the farm would lower down food for them at night and take up the waste. And periodically, she would let my youngest sister, which was-- she was still a little girl, come into the house and play with her little girl. They could not come out.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a Polish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a Ukrainian family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukrainian family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe Polish, I don't know. It was Polish probably. At any rate, this is how they survived. During the course, when they were in that hole, she promised God that if she survives with her family, she will do something good for somebody. And I was that somebody. So when she found me, [DRINKS] she fulfilled the promise that she made to God.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took me in, made me part of her family and treated me like her own child, like her own son. On top of that, they had three daughters. And they always wanted a son. So they kept me. And then they during-- they asked me one time if I would want to be adopted and carry their name. And I told them no, I would like to carry my father's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they understood. And they respected me for that. And this is how it-- how it stayed. From there, later on, we moved to Lodz. And I moved with the-- I was part of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they go to Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the first step to-- to go to Germany, to go to the United States. It was the first step of going-- reaching the United States. So from there, we went to Lodz. And we stayed in Lodz for maybe a year or maybe more than a year. Then my mother had connections. And she made arrangements for somebody to take us across the border at night, illegal, to cross the German border. And we got to Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you got across the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: At night, walking. And there was a guide that my mother paid him for it. And the whole family, plus two friends of hers, we all crossed the border. And we went to Berlin. When we got to Berlin, we went to a Jewish camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I think we went to Jewish camps. Or, it had something to do with the Jewish organizations. And from there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember which organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: If it would be any organization, it would be UNRRA.

speaker: interviewer
text: UNRRA?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. From there, they send [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the children, the two older sisters and myself, they send us to Eschwege, to a DP camp first. Then my mother, my father, and the younger sister came later, to the same camp. Why they send us first, I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was life like in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the DP camp? It was-- it was pretty good. I-- I went to an auto mechanic school. And I learned how to be an auto mechanic. And I worked in a garage. And then I didn't like it, because it was-- I could always smell the gasoline. I could never keep myself clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- got a job as driving a truck. And then I got a job as driving a mail-- mail truck, which was made out of an ambulance. And I used to go to [INAUDIBLE] and pick up mail and bring it to the camp. And we used to-- I made friends there. And we used to go into the city and sometime chase girls and go to movies. [LAUGHS] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I also did some black market transportation with my ambulance, since it was a mail ambulance. And I made some side money. And I bought my first suit that I paid for-- for it myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah. [LAUGHS] And then what happened, Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] Then, after the whole family was together, I told my mother that I want to go to Israel. And she told me no. We're going to find your uncle. And you're going to come with us to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had an uncle in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I knew I had an uncle in America. But I didn't know where. It was my mother's brother. She helped me by going to different organizations to find my uncle. And I was in touch with him. And he's-- and I came here on a youth visa. And my uncle guaranteed, signed for me, that I will not depend on the government for support. And he guaranteed that if anybody's going to support me, it's him, if I can't support myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1948 [INAUDIBLE] in the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did I come in?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- no. To your uncle--

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did I stay?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: East Chicago, Indiana. It's 35 miles from Chicago. And I stayed with my uncle a year. After I was with him a couple months, I couldn't speak English. My cousin's husband had a pants factory. So he gave me my first job as a helper, as a whatever. And my first paycheck was $48 a week. And then I was in touch with my family. And I found out that my older sister was in DC, settled in DC. And my parents settled in Brooklyn, New York. My other sister settled in Jersey. So I decided I wanted to go visit my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came to DC, to visit my sister. And I liked it so much. And then I also found a couple of people from my hometown that came to the United States much earlier. Before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Way before the war. And I met with them. And they questioned me about my hometown and about their family. At any rate, I liked DC so much that I decided I wanted to move. So I went back, and I told my uncle that I'm going to quit the job. And I'm moving to DC. And he was devastated. The night before I left, he stayed up with me until 2 o'clock in the morning, preaching to me, that how you can get in trouble at night, hanging out in the street. And how you can, as a young boy, you can get in trouble in this and get in trouble with that. And I assured him that I'm not going to get in trouble. And I'm going to try to work and make something out of myself. [DRINKS] And this is what happened. I moved here. The people from my hometown got me a job, working in a cleaning plant. From there, I worked in a cleaning store. And from there, after that, I was drafted into the army. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- I believe it was in 1950, I was drafted in the army. And I was-- I came to Fort Meade, in Maryland. And the night I was checked in, I was terribly upset, devastated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The reason that I was upset is because I thought it was very unfair for them to draft me in the army and send me to another war when I first came from one fire. And I was scared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So they send me to the rabbi, to the Jewish rabbi in the service.

speaker: interviewer
text: The chaplain?

speaker: interviewee
text: The chaplain. And he talked to me, and calmed me down and kind of made me feel at home and explained to me why I was drafted and why I have to go. And he kind of settled me down a little bit. And after a couple days, I was settled. And I-- I was-- then they send me for basic training. And I didn't-- I didn't have any problem later on. The first couple nights is when I had a big problem. I was terribly upset. And I thought that it was unfair. I'm not even a citizen, and they drafted me. And they want to send me in the Korean War. And here I just came from-- from this war. And I went through all kind of hardship. And I felt it was unfair. But after a couple nights, I settled down. And I went through basic training. And I was shipped out to Korea. Before I was shipped out to Korea, I went to visit-- I had leave. And I went to visit my parents in Brooklyn. And then I reported back in the camp. And they shipped me out to Korea, after-- after basic training. And I was on the front line for probably three months. I have made a close buddy. I had a close friend of mine that we went through basic training. He was from Pittsburgh. He was not Jewish. And we became buddies. And both of us were shipped out the same time. And we wound up on the-- on the front line, in the same place. And one night, we had dinner together. And he had duty that night. And I-- I was on the other side of the hill. And I went into the bunker. And the next morning, I found out he got killed. So-- [DRINKS] after that, I got transferred to the motor pool. And I got assigned to a jeep. It was company commander's jeep that I was driving for. And I would also do errands. And one day, I was driving up the hill towards the front line. And there was one area that was zeroed in by the enemy. And it was covered with nets, green nets. And my jeep got hit. And when I got to the-- to the destination-- I was going, on top of the hill-- I realized that the windshield on the passenger side had a bullet hole. Luckily, there was nobody in the jeep but me. That's how close I got. And among other things, that-- that day, that's how close I got to get killed. And after that, I was assigned to a truck outfit, moving troops. And I drove a 2 and 1/2 ton truck for a while. And this is how my army life was. We-- I spent 12 months in Korea. And then they sent us back to the States. And we came back to the States in 1953, in May of 1953, the draftees had three months left of service. So the army decided to give us an early discharge. They-- they didn't need us anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got an early discharge. And I went to my parents in Brooklyn first. Then I decided to settle in DC.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went back to DC, and I got a job. I rented a room from a lady. And this lady, her sister worked for a chain store called Giant Food. And her sister told me that she'd be able to get me a job with this company. And that's what she did. And I worked as a grocery-- I started as a grocery clerk and left as assistant manager with a promise that if I don't quit the job, I'll be on the manager trainee program within months. But I already decided to go in business for myself. And I had a deposit on the store. And this was always my goal, to go in business for myself. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I bought a small grocery store. And I stayed there three years. I ran the store for three years. And then I have to have a hernia operation.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had nobody to take care of the store. Kids were small. So I decided to sell the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were married, Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have four children, two boys and two girls. So anyway, after three years, I had to have an operation. I had nobody to take care of the store. So I sold the store. And I had the operation. I recuperated. Then I bought another store, which was bigger. And I stayed there for 27 years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I sent three of my children to college. My youngest son didn't-- was not into books. He barely finished high school. He wanted-- he's very good with his hands. And this is what he wanted to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wound up to be an auto mechanic. My oldest son is in computer. My youngest-- oldest daughter is in computer, college graduate. And they're doing extremely well. [DRINKS] And I'm very proud of them. [DRINKS] Mark, in those terrible years when you lost your family and then fled and lived under a false identity, what do you think helped you survive? What influenced you? What helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the main thing, the main person that influenced me is the man that I stayed with for two-- two nights. And he talked to me for three of four hours the night before he told me I had to leave. And he assured me that I'm going to survive. And he told me a lot of things that was going to happen to me in the future. And the reason I had confidence in him, because he was a very religious man, well-read up on the Bible. He had Bible sayings on the walls, framed in. And for some reason, I felt very comfortable with him. And he sounded so sincere. The main thing that made me feel comfortable with him is that he told me that he wants-- he would like for me to stay with him. He wants-- he would like-- he would like to hide me from the Germans. But he was very scared, because in case he get caught. And he gave me a lot of confidence for the future of working and looking for work or whatever. And the second person that helped me a great deal was my second mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] I need to stop. Can we stop for a minute?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mark, you were telling me about your feelings about your mother-- and what she gave-- your second mother-- and what she gave to you. Would you continue with that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- other than the man that I just talked about, my second mother gave me a lot of love and understanding and a home that helped me pull out from the devastation that I went through and wind up with-- without any hangups and gave me determination to make something out of myself. She was second-- she was a lady. She was second to none in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] Her name was Tobi Davis. My father's name was Edmund Davis. My sisters was Lisa, Edith, and Anne. Lisa was the oldest. Anne was the youngest. My youngest son is named after my second mother. My oldest son is named after my real father. My youngest daughter is named after my mother. And my oldest daughter is named after my step sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: The sister you lost in the Ukraine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the parents you lost in Ukraine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then your second mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How wonderful. Mark, this-- your-- your story is incredible. What would you like to tell others about what you went through? And what would you like to tell others?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] In short, in short, I would like to point out to the-- to the youth [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that if you have will and determination, you can always make something out of yourself. And this is what my experience was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the other thing that I would like to say to the young generation today is drugs and violence is not a way of life. Peace and determination and hard work will make something out of yourself. They can make something out of themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: You, as a child, had everything happen to you. You lost your family. You lost the years of education.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lost--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have a teenage life. I didn't have a school life. But I was determined. And part of this determination is thanks to my second mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And with that determination and will, I made something out of myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes you did. Thank you so much for sharing your-- your thoughts with us. We really appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mark, would you introduce us to these young people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest son, Ira. This is my oldest daughter, Rochelle. This is Rochelle's husband, Gary. Then I have a younger daughter, lives in Israel. And she's married. Her name is Esther. Then I have another son. He's the baby of the family. And he lives in Brandywine, Maryland, which is near Clinton. And he couldn't come for reasons that I don't want to talk about. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you from, Ira?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm from Chicago. And I came in, because I wanted to hear the-- hear the tape and hear what he had to say. It's-- it's-- it's important to make sure that we capture the story. Because without him telling the story, details and nuances, the story'd get lost. And being the oldest, I've heard the story so many times that there are parts of it that I can recite. And there are parts of it that are new to me today. And there are parts that I learned in just listening to what he had to say. So by allowing him to say the story and capture it this way, it gets captured properly, so that everybody can hear it in the future including my children and their children, over time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two. I have a boy that's seven and a daughter that's four.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Teddy and Ariel. So this will-- this will allow me to make sure that they properly understand what happened, and the story doesn't get lost over time. That by being able to see this in the future, and they can show it to their children someday, they will be able to preserve the story. And they will carry on as time goes on. The clarity of the story doesn't get lost as time marches by. The other-- the other thing that I'm hopeful of is that there's a-- there's a concept about the Holocaust that, at least, I never really hear talked about too much. And there's plenty of discussion about those that lost their lives. But there's something else that got lost in that there's portions of the family tree that get fractured away, because people were killed. And people were lost. And I'm hopeful that by doing this and by putting this together, perhaps someday we'll find parts of the family that we have not been able to find over time. It's only in recent years that we've located members of the family that are far distant on his mother's side. But I don't believe that we've been successful in finding anybody on his father's side.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: So perhaps this will be one way, where maybe one day they'll see this and be able to contact us.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would be wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shelly, where are you from?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live in Pasadena, Maryland. And I came down for the interview came down yesterday. Gary and I flew down, especially for the interview. We felt it was important to be here for the interview. And we knew that it was important to dad, to-- for us to be here to join him as part of the interview. And also to hear the story, too. A lot of the pieces of the story, you hear bits and pieces. But to hear it all together is very special.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you brought your husband with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I came because, Mark asked us to. I know it meant a lot to him. And I just wanted to tell him that personally, I felt honored that he invited us. And I enjoyed sharing this experience with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's wonderful that you're here. It's-- that's just great. Is there anything else you'd like to say, Mark?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just want to thank my children for coming here and supporting me. And I'm sorry that the others couldn't be here. But they-- they're with me in their own way.

speaker: interviewer
text: They'll be getting the tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And certainly sharing it with you.
